samples_data.alert_text: TCP flow on port 8080/HTTP-Proxy Volume: 7 packets total (3 forward / 4 backward), 455 bytes total (326 B sent, 129 B received). Byte ratio received/sent: 0.4x (client-dominant). Duration: 9.3 ms. Inter-arrival time (IAT): mean 1.6 ms, std 3.3 ms, max 8.3 ms. Forward IAT: mean 0.3 ms, max 0.6 ms. Forward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 326 B, std 188.2 B. Backward packet sizes: min 0 B, max 112 B, std 53.8 B. TCP flags: PSHx1, RSTx1, ECEx1. TCP window: client init window 8192 B (non-standard/custom stack); server init window 219 B. Minimum forward segment size: 20 B. Analysis hints: SYN flood: NOT possible (SYN count = 0); Flow Duration: 9309.0 (unusually low, z=-9.9); Flow IAT Mean: 1551.5 (unusually low, z=-9.9).
samples_data.label: Bot
samples_data.output_text:
This alert indicates a potential Slowloris Partial HTTP Connection DoS attack or a similar slow-and-low DoS technique that uses minimal bandwidth to exhaust server resources by keeping connections open with partial headers.
The extremely low flow duration (9.3 ms) and inter-arrival time mean (1551.5 ms), combined with the client-dominant byte ratio, are consistent with Slowloris or a similar slow DoS technique. The non-standard TCP window size further supports this hypothesis.
retrieved_contexts:
Behavioral Rule: Slowloris Partial HTTP Connection DoS
Protocol: TCP | Ports: dst_port:80,443,8080
Keywords: Slowloris, slow DoS, partial HTTP, connection exhaustion, low bandwidth DoS, slow headers, keep-alive abuse
MITRE ATT&CK: Impact | T1499 — Endpoint Denial of Service (T1499.001 - OS Exhaustion Flood)
Detection: Very long flow duration: minutes to hours; Very low packet rate: slow inter-arrival time; Small forward packets: incomplete HTTP headers sent in fragments; High flow IAT mean with low packet count; Standard TCP window: legitimate OS stack; Connection kept alive with minimal traffic
Flow profile: direction=outbound_to_server; duration=very_long (minutes to hours); payload=minimal (partial headers only); handshake=complete; byte_ratio=client_dominant but very low volume; packet_pattern=sparse_periodic (long IAT, few packets); tcp_flags=normal, no FIN for extended period
Classtype: denial-of-service
Severity: Medium
Context: Slowloris holds HTTP connections open by sending partial HTTP request headers very slowly, never completing the request. Each connection occupies a server thread until the server's connection pool is exhausted. Unlike volumetric DoS, Slowloris uses minimal bandwidth, making it hard to detect by rate-based thresholds. Key indicators are extremely long flow duration (minutes) combined with very low packet rates to HTTP ports.
Differential diagnosis: Slowloris (BHR-006) never completes the HTTP request header — it keeps sending partial header lines. SlowHTTPTest (BHR-007) completes the header but sends the POST body very slowly. Both are slow-and-low, but Slowloris has LESS total data transferred because no body is ever sent. Compared to Hulk/GoldenEye (BHR-005), Slowloris has far fewer packets and much longer duration.

Behavioral Rule: High-Volume DDoS Flood — HOIC Application Layer
Protocol: TCP | Ports: dst_port:80,443,8080,8443
Keywords: DDoS, HOIC, High Orbit Ion Cannon, distributed flood, volumetric, booster scripts, coordinated attack, application flood
MITRE ATT&CK: Impact | T1498 — Network Denial of Service (T1498.001 - Direct Network Flood)
Detection: Extremely high packet rate and byte volume; Many concurrent connections to same destination port; Short burst flows with high packet count; Server-dominant or balanced byte ratio at scale; Traffic from multiple source IPs targeting the same destination; Standard TCP window: uses legitimate OS stacks
Flow profile: direction=outbound_to_server; duration=short_per_flow; payload=medium_to_large (booster script payloads); handshake=complete; byte_ratio=balanced_or_server_dominant_at_scale; packet_pattern=extreme_volume_short_bursts; tcp_flags=normal HTTP cycle
Classtype: denial-of-service
Severity: High
Context: High Orbit Ion Cannon (HOIC) and similar application-layer DDoS tools generate massive HTTP request volumes from multiple sources using booster scripts to amplify traffic. Individual flows may look like normal HTTP requests but the aggregate connection rate is abnormally high. HOIC uses randomized headers and booster scripts to generate varied request patterns that are harder to filter. Severity is High because the objective is to take the target service offline through distributed coordination.
Differential diagnosis: HOIC DDoS (BHR-008) comes from MULTIPLE source IPs (distributed) and has higher aggregate volume than single-source Hulk/GoldenEye (BHR-005). BHR-008 may show balanced or server-dominant byte ratio because booster scripts trigger large server responses. SYN Flood (BHR-009) does not complete the handshake and has zero payload, while HOIC uses complete TCP connections with actual HTTP content.

Behavioral Rule: Zero-Byte One-Directional Flow — Port Probe or Scan
Protocol: TCP
Keywords: zero byte, one-directional, port probe, liveness check, filtered port, no response, silent probe
MITRE ATT&CK: Reconnaissance | T1595 — Active Scanning (T1595.001 - Scanning IP Blocks)
Detection: Zero bytes transferred in both directions; Only forward packets: server did not respond; Very short flow duration; SYN count = 0: not a standard connection initiation; Non-standard TCP window: custom or malware stack
Flow profile: direction=outbound; duration=very_short; payload=zero; handshake=none; byte_ratio=zero; packet_pattern=single_packet_no_response; tcp_flags=varies (no SYN, possibly ACK or other)
Classtype: network-scan
Severity: Low
Context: Zero-byte one-directional flows with no server response indicate either a filtered/closed port or a probe that was blocked. When combined with a non-standard TCP window size, this is consistent with botnet reachability probes or port liveness checks rather than legitimate traffic. The absence of SYN rules out normal connection initiation. Standalone this is low severity, but patterns of such flows from the same source indicate systematic probing.
Differential diagnosis: Zero-byte probe (BHR-021) has NO specific flag pattern and NO server response. BHR-001 specifically uses ACK flag on C2 ports. SYN scan (BHR-017) uses SYN flag. BHR-021 is the most generic probe indicator — it catches probes that don't fit the specific patterns of other rules.

Rule: ET DOS HTTP GET AAAAAAAA Likely FireFlood
Protocol: TCP
Detection: 'GET AAAAAAAA HTTP/1.1'
Severity: Major

Rule: ET DOS Excessive Large Tree Connect Response
Protocol: TCP | Ports: src_port:445
Severity: Major
Reference: url,isc.sans.edu/forums/diary/Windows+SMBv3+Denial+of+Service+Proof+of+Concept+0+Day+Exploit/22029/
samples_data.reference:
Short TCP session to HTTP-Proxy port 8080 lasting 9.3 ms with 7 packets and 455 bytes (client-dominant), terminated by RST. The non-standard initial window of 8192 B identifies a custom (non-browser) TCP stack, and the PSH-data-then-RST pattern is consistent with botnet C2 beaconing or proxy-tunneled check-in where the implant posts a small payload and tears the connection down to evade flow-based detection.
Behavioral fingerprint (custom TCP stack, micro-burst with PSH+RST, proxy port) matches malware check-in traffic; if confirmed, the source host is already under remote control and exfiltration or command retrieval is in progress.
